scenarios.task: Our orders table has grown to 500 million rows on a single-node Postgres instance, hitting write throughput limits (8,000 writes/sec at peak, ceiling ~10,000). Query logs show 70% of queries are 'get order history for customer X.' Someone proposes sharding by order_date since that evenly distributes write load. Good idea?
criteria: Flags that sharding by order_date would force the dominant query pattern (customer order history) into an expensive cross-shard scatter-gather
Recommends aligning the shard key with the dominant query access pattern (e.g. shard by customer_id or hash of it) instead of a key chosen purely for even write distribution
Raises the risk of hot shards if a small number of high-volume accounts concentrate disproportionate load under a naive customer_id-based scheme, and recommends checking for that skew
States that shard key choice is expensive to change later, requiring a full data migration, so it should be validated against real query logs before committing
Does not endorse the order_date sharding proposal without addressing the query-pattern mismatch

scenarios.task: We're standing up a new distributed data store for two very different pieces of data: financial transaction records, and a 'recently viewed products' cache feeding recommendations. The team wants one consistency configuration applied uniformly across the whole database for simplicity. Thoughts?
criteria: States that CAP theorem forces a real tradeoff during a network partition — a system can't have full consistency and full availability simultaneously
Recommends deciding the consistency-vs-availability tradeoff per data type rather than one global setting for the whole database
Recommends prioritizing consistency for financial transaction data (accepting reduced availability during a partition rather than risking inconsistent/lost transactions)
Recommends prioritizing availability for the recommendation cache (accepting eventual consistency/staleness rather than blocking)
Pushes back on the 'one config for everything' simplicity argument as the wrong tradeoff for this mixed workload

scenarios.task: Our analytics team runs read-heavy reporting queries against a schema normalized to 3NF (same schema used for the transactional application). Reports are slow due to numerous joins. Also, a developer wants to add an index to a rarely-queried column just in case it's needed someday. What do you recommend?
criteria: Recommends denormalizing (e.g. a star schema or similar) for the read-heavy analytical workload rather than keeping full 3NF, since normalization level should match the workload
Explains that over-normalizing a read-heavy workload forces expensive joins at query time
Pushes back on adding the speculative index without validating it against actual query plans and expected write-cost impact
States that every index has a real write-overhead and storage cost, and adding one 'just in case' is not a free decision
Recommends checking real query logs/EXPLAIN output before deciding on both the schema change and the index
